title: Your Claude Code Carousels Suck (Here's The Fix)
summary: Chase AI demonstrates a hybrid approach to creating social media carousels by combining Claude Code's HTML generation with Higgsfield's CLI for AI image generation. The technique addresses the quality issues of purely Claude-generated carousels by integrating image generation to create more polished, non-generic outputs.
writeup: This tutorial diagnoses why Claude Code carousels fail on social media (they are all pure HTML and look identical) and proposes a two-part hybrid fix: use an external AI image model for the cover slide, then use Claude Code HTML assets for the lower-stakes body slides. The presenter demonstrates the full workflow live, generating a cover with GPT Images 2 via the Higgsfield CLI, a tool that unifies DALL-E, Midjourney, and GPT Images 2 behind one terminal interface. A carousel the presenter built this way reached hundreds of thousands of views and 18,000 likes, making the case that the cover image is the decisive conversion lever.
key_points: The core diagnosis is that Claude Code carousels saturate feeds because they are rendered as raw HTML slide decks with no visual differentiation; the cover image is the only slide that must be visually striking enough to stop a scroll, so that is the one place to spend money and iteration time on a real AI image generator.

The hybrid workflow splits cost and effort deliberately: the cover is generated with GPT Images 2 or Nana Banana Pro at 2K quality and a 4:5 aspect ratio, while the remaining body and value slides are built cheaply as Claude Code HTML assets, keeping the system repeatable and fast.

Higgsfield CLI (installed from higgsfield.ai) provides a single terminal interface for generating images across DALL-E, Midjourney, and GPT Images 2, so switching image models requires only a prompt change rather than a new account or UI.

Cover image generation is inherently iterative: the presenter requested four image variants from a single prompt, selected the best, then submitted a follow-up pass to add the title text with a rainbow gradient, expecting and accepting that one-shotting a strong cover is rare.

Building a personal carousel folder and accumulating 30 to 60 finished carousels creates a reusable template library: once 10 to 15 formats are proven, new carousels can be created by instructing Claude Code to replicate a numbered prior template on a new topic.

Cross-niche inspiration research on Instagram or TikTok (spending 20 to 30 minutes and screenshotting both the cover slide and a body slide of carousels that catch the eye) is the prerequisite step before any generation, because starting from a blank slate without a reference image produces weaker prompts and weaker results.
